intro:
The Fall refers to the primal transgression of Adam and Eve recorded in Genesis 3 — their disobedience of God's command not to eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil (Gen. 2:17; 3:1-7). The serpent's temptation followed a pattern of questioning God's word, impugning God's motives, and offering autonomous moral authority ('you will be like God, knowing good and evil'). The eating of the forbidden fruit resulted in immediate spiritual consequences — shame, fear, hiding from God — and divine judgment: the ground was cursed, labor became toilsome, childbirth painful, the serpent was placed under the woman's enmity, and both were expelled from the garden and its tree of life (Gen. 3:14-24).

The theological consequences of the Fall as developed by Paul are immense. His two-Adam Christology (Rom. 5:12-21; 1 Cor. 15:21-22, 45-49) presents the Fall as introducing sin and death into the entire human family: 'just as sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and in this way death came to all people, because all sinned' (Rom. 5:12). This pattern is reversed by Christ's obedience and resurrection. The theological categories of original sin (the universal human inheritance of a sinful nature from Adam), total depravity (sin's comprehensive corruption of human nature), and the need for divine redemption all find their Pauline foundations in the Fall narrative. The promise embedded within the judgment — that the Seed of the woman will crush the serpent's head (Gen. 3:15), traditionally called the protoevangelium — is read as the first messianic prophecy and the organizing thread of all subsequent redemptive history.
